ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chicken wing food product class; no specific entry for shrimp-paste-marinated crispy fried chicken wings. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for shrimp-paste-marinated fried chicken wings. A00LC (chicken meat) used as closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of 3 crispy shrimp paste chicken wings weighs approximately 180 g, typical of a restaurant appetiser or side dish portion as served at Singaporean Chinese restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Fried chicken wing, with skin — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05062) adapted with shrimp paste marinade adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central; supplemented with HPB Singapore nutrient reference for har cheong gai
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base values derived from USDA FoodData Central fried chicken wing with skin (FDC ID 171477). Sodium adjusted upward to reflect shrimp paste (belacan) marinade contribution, consistent with HPB Singapore dietary data for har cheong gai. Shrimp paste adds approximately 200–300 mg sodium per 100 g to the finished product. Final values represent a best-estimate composite for this dish category.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fried chicken and battered poultry products — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for battered/fried meat products and mixed protein-fat-carbohydrate dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for shrimp-paste-marinated crispy fried chicken wings. GI estimated at 45 (low category) based on analogous fried battered chicken products in the University of Sydney GI Database. The thin wheat-flour batter contributes modest carbohydrate; the high protein and fat content of the chicken wing matrix substantially attenuates glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 12.2) / 100 ≈ 5.5, where carbohydrate per 180 g serving = 6.8 × 1.8 = 12.24 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from shrimp paste (belacan) marinade
hypertension.guidance: Each 180 g serving provides approximately 1224 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and frequency of consumption of this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load with high fat content affecting insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI (estimated 45) and moderate glycemic load (GL ≈ 5.5 per serving) are relatively favourable for blood glucose management, but the high total fat content (approximately 35 g per serving) may impair insulin sensitivity with regular consumption; portion control is advised for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium, saturated fat, cholesterol, and deep-frying oil absorption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is high in sodium (≈1224 mg per serving), saturated fat (≈9.4 g per serving), and cholesterol (≈158 mg per serving) due to deep-frying with skin and shrimp paste marinade; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish only occasionally and in small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Chicken Wings Marinated in Shrimp Paste (脆皮虾酱鸡翅), known colloquially in Singapore as Har Cheong Gai, is a popular Singaporean-Chinese fried chicken dish in which whole chicken wings are marinated in fermented shrimp paste (belacan or har cheong), typically combined with garlic, sesame oil, rice wine, sugar, and white pepper, then coated in a thin wheat-flour-and-egg batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The result is a dish with an intensely savoury, umami-rich flavour profile derived from the fermented shrimp paste, a thin crackling crust, and juicy, tender meat inside. The dish is a staple of Singaporean zi char (cooked food) restaurants and is also served as a dim sum item or appetiser at Chinese restaurants such as Din Tai Fung. Nutritionally, a 3-piece serving (approximately 180 g) provides around 522 kcal, 40.5 g protein, 35.1 g fat, and 12.2 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is notably high at approximately 1224 mg per serving due to the shrimp paste marinade, making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low category), and the glycemic load per serving is approximately 5.5, which is relatively moderate for blood glucose management. The dish contains crustacean, gluten, egg, and sesame allergens. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal-certified food without specific certification.

confidence_reason: No exact nutrient entry for shrimp-paste-marinated crispy fried chicken wings was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central fried chicken wing with skin (FDC ID 171477) with sodium adjusted upward to account for shrimp paste marinade contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for battered fried poultry. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central fried chicken wing with skin (FDC ID 171477) with sodium increased by approximately 250 mg per 100 g to reflect shrimp paste marinade, consistent with published belacan sodium contribution data. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for fried chicken wing used as the base reference. 3. GI is an analogous estimate (45, low) based on battered fried chicken products in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for har cheong gai was identified in peer-reviewed literature. 4. Halal status is marked false as shrimp paste (belacan) may contain non-halal processing agents depending on manufacturer; consumers requiring halal certification should verify with the specific restaurant. 5. Serving size of 180 g (3 pieces) is an estimate based on typical restaurant portion weights for chicken wings (approximately 55–65 g per mid-joint wing piece). 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central fried chicken wing data and are approximate.
